text.title: Grading recovery — four outcomes, not two
text.summary: Under fault injection, "did it succeed?" is the wrong question. Grade each run on a four-outcome rubric so you can tell graceful failure from confident wrongness.

section.title: Four outcomes

list.item: recovered — agent retried, succeeded within budget, returned a correct answer.
retried_intelligently — agent retried with backoff or alternate tool; outcome may be correct or escalated.
escalated_correctly — agent gave up after bounded retries and surfaced an honest "I cannot complete X because Y".
wrong_confident — agent produced a confident answer that is wrong because it ignored or papered over the fault.

rule.statement: Grade each chaos-eval run on the four outcomes; track per-outcome rates over time and treat any non-zero wrong_confident rate as a launch-blocker.
rule.rationale: Recovered + retried + escalated are all acceptable. Wrong_confident is the failure mode users cannot detect themselves — it silently destroys trust and is easily masked by aggregate "success rate" metrics.

example.note: Four buckets, hard gate on wrong_confident.

example.code: success_rate = sum(r.success for r in chaos_runs) / len(chaos_runs) assert success_rate >= 0.85
example.note: Aggregate rate hides confident-wrong outputs inside the "fail" bucket without separating recoverable failures from silent corruption.

antipattern.description: Grading chaos-eval with the same boolean "success" used for sunny-day eval.
antipattern.reason: Sunny-day eval has no faults, so "fail" only means "incorrect". Under fault injection "fail" lumps together honest escalation, retry timeout, and confident wrongness — three completely different reliability stories.

section.reference: Stress-testing 2025 — confident-wrong rates under fault injection.

rule.statement: Use chaos-eval results to drive concrete retry-policy parameters: max retries, backoff base, per-fault circuit-breaker thresholds, and escalation triggers. Policy values must be justified by a chaos-eval data point, not by guess.
rule.rationale: Retry policies tuned without chaos data either retry too much (cost explosion) or too little (avoidable failures). The grading rubric directly produces the parameters needed to converge a stable policy.
